name: stream-chain
description: Stream-JSON chaining for multi-agent pipelines, data transformation, and sequential workflows
capabilities: Execute custom prompt sequences where each step receives full context from previous outputs
Run predefined pipelines for analysis, refactoring, testing, and optimization
Configure timeout settings and debug modes for complex workflows
Define reusable custom pipelines in configuration files
Integrate with swarm coordination for multi-agent scenarios
Store chain context in memory for cross-session persistence
author: ruvnet
securitySummary: All 118 static findings are false positives. This is a pure documentation file (SKILL.md) containing only markdown documentation for a CLI workflow orchestration tool. There is no executable code, no scripts, no network calls, no filesystem access, no environment variable reads, and no command execution capabilities. The static scanner incorrectly flagged markdown code fences as shell execution, hex strings as C2 indicators, and common words as cryptographic references.
valueStatement: Building complex multi-step AI workflows is difficult when each step cannot easily receive context from previous results. Stream-Chain enables sophisticated pipelines where each agent's output automatically flows into the next step, supporting custom chains and predefined battle-tested workflows.
userTitle: Chain multi-agent workflows with streaming data flow
limitations: Requires minimum 2 prompts for custom chains
Context limited to approximately 100K tokens per step
Per-step timeout defaults to 30 seconds but can be configured
No built-in error recovery for failed intermediate steps

useCases.title: Multi-step code development
useCases.description: Build features through sequential steps: implement, validate, test, and document with full context flow

useCases.title: Automated security audits
useCases.description: Run security scanning chains that identify, categorize, and propose fixes for vulnerabilities

useCases.title: Data transformation pipelines
useCases.description: Process data through extraction, normalization, validation, and reporting stages

promptTemplates.title: Basic chain
promptTemplates.prompt: Use stream-chain to run "Write a sorting function" "Add unit tests to the function"

promptTemplates.title: Analysis pipeline
promptTemplates.prompt: Run the analysis pipeline to examine the codebase structure and identify improvement areas

promptTemplates.title: Refactor workflow
promptTemplates.prompt: Execute the refactor pipeline to identify code smells and create a prioritized improvement plan

promptTemplates.title: Custom security chain
promptTemplates.prompt: Run a custom chain: "Scan for SQL injection risks" "Identify authentication flaws" "Generate security test cases" with 60 second timeout

bestPractices: Use clear, specific prompts that build logically on previous outputs
Configure appropriate timeouts based on task complexity (30-120 seconds)
Include verification steps to validate intermediate results before continuing
antiPatterns: Using vague prompts like "check security" instead of specific targets
Skipping validation steps after complex transformations
Setting timeouts too low for analysis-heavy tasks

faq.question: What tools support stream-chain?
faq.answer: Claude, Codex, and Claude Code all support stream-chain workflows through claude-flow CLI

faq.question: How many steps can a chain have?
faq.answer: Chains support unlimited steps but context is limited to approximately 100K tokens per step

faq.question: Can I combine stream-chain with other skills?
faq.answer: Yes, stream-chain integrates with swarm coordination and memory management for advanced workflows

faq.question: Is data sent externally?
faq.answer: Stream-chain operates within the AI tool's environment; no external data transmission occurs

faq.question: What if a step fails?
faq.answer: Currently failed steps terminate the chain; configure timeouts appropriately and include validation steps

faq.question: How does stream-chain differ from swarm?
faq.answer: Stream-chain runs steps sequentially with full context flow; swarm runs agents in parallel for concurrent execution
